title: Men Are Such Beasts

jokes.text: How come I never understand a single word you say, yet I always know what you're talking about?
jokes.setup: Tony has just had a full conversation with Latka about relationship advice
jokes.punchline: Tony realizes the paradox that Latka's gibberish somehow makes perfect sense
jokes.explanation: Perfect character-specific humor that plays on Latka's established trait of speaking incomprehensibly yet being understood. Well-earned observational comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. I understood every word you just said.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Of course you did.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No, I mean I actually understood it. The thing is... you weren't speaking English.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: So?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So? So how is that possible? How did I understand a word you said?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Because you listen with your heart, not your ears.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Oh, come on. That's not an answer.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Isn't it?

jokes.text: Latka's non-verbal response and Tony's 'Yeah, that must be it'
jokes.setup: Tony asks a direct question about communication
jokes.punchline: Latka responds with more gibberish that Tony accepts as a valid explanation
jokes.explanation: Extends the previous joke's logic. Tony's acceptance of nonsense as explanation is the comedy beat here.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Schibida-dop-dop, flibberdy-skee, munchka-dunchka-zoo!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Oh, that makes sense.

jokes.text: Alex Rieger, 180,000 miles without an accident, voted 'New York Cabbie of the Month' August 1977, just rams into a parked car?
jokes.setup: Alex's impressive driving credentials are listed
jokes.punchline: The ironic contrast with his current accident
jokes.explanation: Standard ironic setup about reputation vs. reality. Functional but predictable structure.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Look at this - Alex was a stunt driver, drove in three movies, won a precision driving award...

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: And now he can't parallel park without hitting a fire hydrant.

jokes.text: You mean, you had a choice of running over some mutt and ramming into a Cordoba? A Chrysler Cordoba with Corinthian leather?
jokes.setup: Alex explains his moral choice between hitting a dog or a car
jokes.punchline: Louie's focus on the luxury car details rather than the ethics
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Louie's warped priorities. The Corinthian leather detail is a nice period-specific touch.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I had to make a choice—hit the dog or hit the car.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What kind of car?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: A Mercedes.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You hit the dog, right?

jokes.text: And you chose... You made a conscious choice to hit the Cordoba? Where are your values, Rieger?
jokes.setup: The moral dilemma of dog vs. car
jokes.punchline: Louie's incredulity that Alex chose the car over the dog, for purely materialistic reasons
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy - Louie's twisted moral compass where property value trumps animal welfare is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: A dog is a living creature. A car is just metal and rubber.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So you're saying the dog's life is worth more than the car?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Of course it is.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Then why'd you run over the dog and keep the car?

jokes.text: Oh, good thinking, Rieger. Otherwise, he might not know where to reach us and we couldn't pay for the damages. Smart, smart.
jokes.setup: Alex mentions leaving a note as required by law
jokes.punchline: Louie's sarcastic 'praise' for Alex doing something that costs them money
jokes.explanation: Standard sarcastic response fitting Louie's character - he's annoyed by Alex's honesty costing money.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Oh, that's beautiful, Alex. That's real beautiful. You leave a note. You follow the law. You cost me money, but you follow the law. I'm so proud of you I could cry.

jokes.text: You have such an elegant way of making a point, you know that?
jokes.setup: Louie has just made a crude comparison about spitting laws
jokes.punchline: Alex's dry, sarcastic response to Louie's vulgarity
jokes.explanation: Nice deadpan sarcasm from Alex. Good economy of words to deflate Louie's crudeness.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, that's the most disgusting thing I've ever heard you say. And I've heard you talk about your bathroom habits.

jokes.text: $700 damage just to avoid hitting a dog. I mean, if you'd have swerved to avoid hitting an old lady, I could understand. I'm not saying I'd agree, but I could understand. But a dog.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Alex's moral choice in the accident
jokes.punchline: Louie's twisted hierarchy of whose life is worth property damage
jokes.explanation: Dark and perfectly in character for Louie. The 'I'm not saying I'd agree' qualifier makes it even funnier and more disturbing.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You hit a guy, you gotta stop. You hit a parked car, you leave a note. You hit a hydrant, you drive away fast.

jokes.text: Well, look at it this way... You're not a virgin anymore.
jokes.setup: Alex lamenting his first accident in six years
jokes.punchline: Bobby's sexual metaphor for having your first accident
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay connecting accident innocence to sexual innocence. Good economy but somewhat predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Hey, don't worry about it. It happens to the best of us. I had my first accident in six years last week.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You know what they say — it's like losing your virginity. You're nervous, you're sweating, and when it's all over, you realize you weren't nearly as good at it as you thought you'd be.

jokes.text: Yeah, I lost my bumper.
jokes.setup: Bobby's virginity metaphor
jokes.punchline: Alex extends the metaphor with automotive double entendre
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that extends Bobby's metaphor. 'Bumper' works both literally and as innuendo. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, Bobby, losing your virginity is like buying your first car. You're nervous, you don't know what you're doing, and you're worried about stalling out.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yeah, I guess that makes sense.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: The key is to remember: keep both hands on the wheel, don't go too fast, and whatever you do, don't forget to check your oil.

jokes.text: I said, 'Why is that man wearing my pajamas?'
jokes.setup: Tony asks what Alex said to break up with his wife
jokes.punchline: Alex reveals he caught her cheating
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion - we expect relationship advice but get a discovery of infidelity. Perfect economy and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What did you say to her when you broke up?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I said, 'I know about you and the other guy.'

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: And what did she say?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: She said, 'Which other guy?'

jokes.text: There doesn't seem to be much point.
jokes.setup: Denise asks to be introduced to Tony's friends
jokes.punchline: Tony's blunt assessment since he's about to dump her
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - Tony's brutal honesty before the breakup. Economical and character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: These are my friends. You're gonna hate them. I hate them. But I see them every day anyway.

jokes.text: It was nice knowing you.
jokes.setup: The group is leaving Tony and Denise alone for the breakup
jokes.punchline: Alex treats it like Denise is about to die rather than just get dumped
jokes.explanation: Nice dark humor treating the breakup like an execution. Good timing as they exit.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Okay everyone, let's give them some privacy. Come on, they need to be alone.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Denise, is there anything you want to say before... before it happens?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Does anyone have a priest?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: We should probably call her family. They'll want to say goodbye.

jokes.text: Help you stay awake, take a few pounds off or do the 100 in 9.7.
jokes.setup: Denise offering Tony pills
jokes.punchline: The increasingly absurd claims about what the pills can do
jokes.explanation: Sets up Denise's pill addiction and provides escalating absurdity about drug effects.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: Take these pills, Tony. They'll change your life.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What do they do?

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: They increase your confidence, improve your memory, and make you irresistible to women.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: Plus they cure baldness, make you taller, and give you the ability to predict the weather.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Come on...

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: They also allow you to speak fluent Portuguese, make your car run on water, and let you fly by flapping your arms.

jokes.text: There might have been some smarter ways of leading up to that, Tony. I mean, some guys would have tried to soften me up with a lot of flattery. Or they might have made up some long, involved story. Not you. You just came right out with it.
jokes.setup: Tony's direct breakup approach
jokes.punchline: Denise's backhanded compliment about his bluntness
jokes.explanation: Sets up the expectation that she'll accept the breakup gracefully, which she immediately subverts.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: You know, Tony, I have to say... I really admire your honesty. Most men would try to soften the blow, but you? You just come right out and say it. That takes a special kind of courage. The kind that doesn't require any tact whatsoever.

jokes.text: That's also why I'm never gonna let you go.
jokes.setup: Denise seeming to accept Tony's honesty and the breakup
jokes.punchline: The complete reversal - she's using his honesty as a reason to stay together
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection that establishes the episode's central conflict. Great structure and character work.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: Wait, you mean you actually told me the truth? That you don't want to see me anymore?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, I did.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: That's the most honest thing you've ever said to me. You really care about me.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What? No, I just said I want to break up.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: Exactly! You're finally being real with me. We should stay together.

jokes.text: They're going to reenact the accident. It's company policy... it's fun.
jokes.setup: Someone asks what they're doing with the box
jokes.punchline: John's matter-of-fact delivery about the absurd reenactment ritual
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan delivery of absurd bureaucracy. The 'it's fun' addition is perfectly dry.

dialogue.speaker: John
dialogue.line: We're recreating the exact moment when the box arrived. It's important for closure.

jokes.text: No cockroaches, Latka. Get that cockroach out of there.
jokes.setup: They're using objects to represent elements of the accident
jokes.punchline: Latka has contributed an actual cockroach to the reenactment
jokes.explanation: Visual gag that plays on Latka's foreignness and the absurdity of the reenactment setup.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What the hell is that?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Is a cockroach. I find in my apartment. I think, what better way to show how accident happen?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You brought a real cockroach to demonstrate the accident?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Yes! Is very realistic. Just like real thing!

jokes.text: All right, I got a baby. We'll use the baby for the dog.
jokes.setup: They need something to represent the dog in the accident
jokes.punchline: Louie casually suggests using a baby doll for this role
jokes.explanation: Dark humor in the casual substitution. Works well with the bureaucratic absurdity of the whole scene.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Use a baby doll. I got one in my office.

jokes.text: Well, I just closed my eyes and tried to pretend it was her doing all that stuff to me. Pretended it was that ugly witch sucking my fingers. I pretended it was that ugly witch blowing in my ear. And I pretended it was that ugly witch with her hand on my knee.
jokes.setup: Tony explains his strategy using the Wicked Witch imagery
jokes.punchline: The increasingly specific and inappropriate witch scenarios
jokes.explanation: Good escalation building tension for the upcoming reversal. The specificity of the witch actions gets progressively funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: See, I figure if I can just get her to think of me like the Wicked Witch of the West, you know? I'm green, I'm ugly, I want to destroy everything she loves...

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Then when I show up at her door with a bucket of water, she'll think 'Oh no, he's gonna melt me!' and she'll be so scared she'll agree to go out with me just to save her life.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Or better yet, I could dress up like the Wicked Witch. That way when she sees me, she'll be so frightened she'll grant me three wishes and anything I want.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Actually, I could corner her in a haunted castle, trap her in a crystal ball, and force her to watch my home movies until she agrees to love me.

jokes.text: And then what? I got to thinking... who cares how ugly the witch is?
jokes.setup: The elaborate witch fantasy to resist temptation
jokes.punchline: Tony realizes even the ugly witch would be appealing
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection and character work. Perfect timing and economy. Shows Tony's weakness while being genuinely funny.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Even the ugly witch would be appealing.

jokes.text: Listen, Banta, I can make life really rough for you if I wanted to, but you're Italian and I'm Italian. So I'm going to tell you just like I'd tell my own brother... You better smarten up, meatball.
jokes.setup: Louie sets up what seems like ethnic solidarity and familial advice
jokes.punchline: The 'advice' is just a crude insult delivered with fake warmth
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - fake sentiment leading to insult. Great misdirection structure and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Listen to me, you're like a brother to me, you know that? I'm gonna give you some advice, some real advice from the heart.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You got a face like a pizza that somebody sat on. You should do something about that.

jokes.text: Hey, you losers hear this? This sweet young bimbo just booked $217. A new Sunshine Cab company record.
jokes.setup: Denise has made a lot of money
jokes.punchline: Louie's backhanded compliment mixing praise with insults
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie - can't give a compliment without multiple insults. Character-appropriate but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Denise, you made a lot of money. That's terrific. You know, for somebody like you, that's really something special. I mean, most people with your... limitations... they'd never get this far.

jokes.text: I'd shake your hand, but I'm afraid I'd get you hot.
jokes.setup: Louie wanting to congratulate Denise
jokes.punchline: His delusional belief in his own sexual magnetism
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie delusion. Good character work though the joke itself is predictable for him.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Denise! I heard the good news. Congratulations on the new job.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: Thank you, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, if you ever need anything - anything at all - you just come see me. I've got connections all over this city.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: That's very kind of you.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: And between you and me, I think you got the job because of me. You know, I mentioned your name to a few people. They probably told their friends, word got around... Women like confidence, Denise. And class. And a man who knows how to treat a lady. Lucky for you, you know me.

jokes.text: You got enough I can give the other guys?
jokes.setup: Learning about the health dangers of amphetamines
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately wants to distribute them to his drivers for profit
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie - health concerns immediately become business opportunities. Dark but character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Wait a minute, wait a minute. You're telling me these things are dangerous? They can cause heart problems, they can cause all kinds of problems?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: How much can I get them for wholesale?

jokes.text: You better take another one.
jokes.setup: Alex complaining about his headache
jokes.punchline: Denise casually suggesting more dangerous pills
jokes.explanation: Sets up Alex's drug reaction but not particularly clever on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I have a terrible headache.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: Have you tried aspirin?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yes, it didn't work.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: Well, try some of the really dangerous pills then.

jokes.text: What headache?
jokes.setup: Alex just took pills for his headache seconds ago
jokes.punchline: The impossibly fast effect, showing he's already high
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Alex's drug sequence. Simple but effective structure and great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Wow, this is amazing! I can already feel it working!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: My headache is completely gone. I can see colors I've never seen before. Is that a new dimension over there?

jokes.text: I don't feel any different then I ever felt. I seem to keep talking, you know? But I've talked before and even if I am talking more now than I usually do, I could stop anytime I want. There, I just stopped. I just stopped again.
jokes.setup: Alex claiming the pills don't affect him
jokes.punchline: His increasingly manic speech proving the opposite
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic structure - Alex denies the effect while demonstrating it perfectly. Great performance piece for the actor.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: The pills don't affect me at all. I'm completely normal. See? I'm fine. Totally fine. Everything is fine. Fine fine fine fine fine. You know what? I feel great! I feel AMAZING! Why does everyone keep staring at me? Did I say something funny? I'm hilarious! I should be on television! I'm the funniest person in this room! In the WORLD! My heart is racing but that's just because I'm excited! Excited about LIFE! We should all be excited! Let's celebrate! Let's dance! Let's reorganize the entire dispatch system RIGHT NOW!

jokes.text: Hi, Bobby! Hi, Elaine! Hi, John! Hi, Louie! ... Right... bye, Louie. Bye, John! Bye, Elaine! Bye, Bobby! Bye, Tony!
jokes.setup: Alex on amphetamines trying to have normal interactions
jokes.punchline: His manic over-greeting and over-farewell to everyone
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy showing drug effects. Functional but relies on performance more than writing craft.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Hey everybody! How are you? You look great! Did you do something different with your hair? I love it! I absolutely love it! We should hang out sometime, really soon, like tomorrow!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Okay, gotta go! This has been the best conversation of my entire life! You're such a good person! I'm so glad we know each other! See you later, buddy! Bye! Have a great day! I mean it! Really great!

jokes.text: ♪ Bye-bye, love, bye-bye, happiness ♪ ♪ Hello, loneliness ♪ ♪ I think I'm going to die. ♪
jokes.setup: Alex's manic behavior continues
jokes.punchline: Breaking into inappropriate song while high
jokes.explanation: Continues the drug sequence with musical absurdity. The song choice is appropriately random.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: ♪ I'm gonna take a greyhound down to L.A., find a starlet with a convertible and a trust fund... ♪

jokes.text: Now, let me think about that. I thought about it. It was nice working with you.
jokes.setup: Tony threatens to quit if Denise isn't fired
jokes.punchline: Louie's instant 'consideration' and dismissal
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - the fake consideration followed by immediate dismissal. Great structure and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: If you don't fire Denise, I'm quitting.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Okay, Tony, let me think about this... No.

jokes.text: That I don't even have to think about. Bye-bye, Bobby.
jokes.setup: Bobby also threatens to quit
jokes.punchline: Louie doesn't even pretend to consider Bobby's value
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the previous joke - Bobby rates even less consideration than Tony.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I'm gonna quit!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Don't let the door hit ya.

jokes.text: Why you quitting? Sounds good enough for me.
jokes.setup: Everyone else quits in solidarity
jokes.punchline: Latka joins without understanding why
jokes.explanation: Perfect Latka behavior - joining group action without comprehension. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I quit too!

jokes.text: Get back, get back, all of you! Get back! Now, listen to me and listen to me good. I'm drawing a line here. Now, you see that line? You see it? Look at it, look at it.
jokes.setup: Louie facing a mass resignation
jokes.punchline: His desperate, theatrical line-drawing performance
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy moment. Louie's desperation manifests in theatrical gestures. Relies on performance.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're all quitting? All of you? Even you, Mario? You're my best driver!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Don't go. Please. I'm begging you. Look at me. I'm on my knees here.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You want more money? I'll give you more money. You want better hours? Done. You want the moon? I'll get you the moon!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Don't leave me like this. I'm nothing without you guys. Nothing! I'm a broken man!

jokes.text: Because if just one of youse steps one foot across that line, I'll have to fire her.
jokes.setup: Louie's threatening tone about crossing the line
jokes.punchline: The 'threat' is actually giving them what they want
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - the dramatic setup suggests punishment but delivers capitulation. Great structure.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You guys are really pushing it. You cross that line, and I'm gonna give you everything you want.

jokes.text: And if you go out to eat, you're going to find me there, too. And if you go to the movies or you go bowling, just look around, because, baby, I'm not giving you up.
jokes.setup: Denise acknowledging she can't work there anymore
jokes.punchline: Her escalating stalking threats
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of stalker behavior. Creates genuine discomfort while remaining comedic.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: I can't work here anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: If you don't rehire me, I'm going to follow you home.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: I'm going to sit outside your apartment.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: I'm going to follow you to work, to the grocery store, to the gym.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: I'll be everywhere you are.

jokes.text: Psychologists, they got a term for people like you. What is it? Uh... Oh, yeah... you got a screw loose.
jokes.setup: Tony invoking psychological authority
jokes.punchline: The 'technical term' is just a common insult
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection structure. Tony's attempt at sophistication deflates into simplicity.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know, there's a technical term for what you are.

dialogue.speaker: Other Character
dialogue.line: What's that?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: A jerk.

jokes.text: I love you when you're like this.
jokes.setup: Tony being aggressively mean to drive her away
jokes.punchline: Denise finding his cruelty attractive
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic reversal - Tony's strategy backfires completely. Great character consistency and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You're a real waste of space, you know that? A complete loser. Nobody wants you around here.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: Oh Tony, that's so... aggressive. Keep going.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What? You've got nothing to offer. You're pathetic.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: Mmm, yes. Be more cruel to me.

jokes.text: Him.
jokes.setup: Denise asking who Tony is in love with
jokes.punchline: Louie pointing to himself as Tony's supposed love interest
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and timing. The single word delivers maximum surprise and absurdity. Excellent setup pay-off.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: Who is Tony in love with?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Me.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm the love of his life.

jokes.text: I prefer to think of it as an alternative lifestyle.
jokes.setup: Denise asking if Louie is homosexual
jokes.punchline: Louie's politically correct terminology for his fake orientation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Louie committing to the bit with unexpected sensitivity to terminology.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm not homosexual, I'm sexually ambiguous.

jokes.text: To get rid of you, I'd marry him.
jokes.setup: Denise accusing them of stooping low with the lie
jokes.punchline: Tony's commitment to go even further to escape her
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that shows Tony's desperation. Great callback to marriage theme and perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Low? Lady, you haven't seen low. I'm willing to commit crimes I haven't even thought of yet to get away from you.

jokes.text: What do you mean, stoop so low?
jokes.setup: Denise calling their homosexual lie 'stooping low'
jokes.punchline: Louie taking personal offense at the implication
jokes.explanation: Nice character beat - Louie's ego won't let the insult slide even in a fake scenario.

dialogue.speaker: Denise
dialogue.line: That's stooping pretty low, isn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Low? What do you mean low? I've done plenty of low things in my life, but this... this is not low for me!

jokes.text: Boy, am I going to be sick tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Alex's entire drug-fueled sequence
jokes.punchline: His moment of clarity about the inevitable crash
jokes.explanation: Good button for the drug sequence. Shows Alex's awareness returning and sets up consequences. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'm flying, man. I'm really flying. Nothing can touch me now.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I feel like I could do anything. I'm invincible!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Wait... wait a minute.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What goes up... has to come down.

tmdb_overview: Tony cannot escape the clutches of a possessive girlfriend.
insight: Escalation comedy drives a middling episode: 43 jokes in 43 minutes but limited standouts.
